text: This video aims to illustrate a technique, high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery in anterior resection for rectal cancer. Surgeons have concerns whether high ligation of the IMA increases the incidence of anastomotic leakage. Recently, we published a high quality meta-analysis and concluded that high ligation of the IMA did not increase the incidence of anastomotic leakage in anterior resection. Now we present a video and show how we usually do. A fifty four year old male patient was diagnosed with rectal cancer. The BMI of this patient was twenty eight point two. The colonoscopy showed a two times two centimeters tumor located in thirteen centimeters from the anal verge, and the biopsy pathology with adenocarcinoma. CT did not find distant metastasis.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic-assisted anterior resection, beginning with the excision of apical lymph nodes. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is then ligated at its root, followed by the ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and left colic artery (LCA). The autonomic nerve is preserved, and the tumor-specific mesorectum is excised.

The surgeon explores the abdominal cavity to locate the tumor, then separates the left natural attachment and lateral peritoneum. A gauze strip is placed for guidance. The medial lateral approach is performed, with the peritoneum incised to allow air entry, which clarifies the surgical space.

The surgeon separates the talt space using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, carefully preserving the super wire hypogastric plexus. Following the separation, the apical lymph nodes are dissected, with the duodenum protected from damage. The dissection is performed slowly using a harmonic scalpel to prevent post-operative lymphatic fistula.

The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its root and separates the attachment between the IMA and the left sympathetic nerve (LSN). The talt space is further extended, revealing the gauze strip used for guidance. The left ureter and left genital vessels are visible. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and left colic artery (LCA) are ligated together.

The surgeon separates the retrorectal space while preserving the inferior hypogastric plexus. The recto sacrofasta is incised, allowing entry into the superior space above the levator ani muscle. The lateral space of the rectum is carefully dissected, ensuring the preservation of the pelvic plexus.

The surgeon uses a five-centimeter silk thread to mark the location for excision. The tumor-specific mesorectum is excised, followed by the transection of the rectum using a stapler. The entire epigastric plexus is visually checked, and the specimen is extracted through a small incision.
The surgeon constructs an end-to-end anastomosis using a double stapling technique. The integrity of the anastomosis is confirmed with an air leak test. A pelvic drainage tube is then placed.

The duration of the surgery was one hundred ten minutes and the blood loss was about twenty milliliters. The patient was discharged on day seven. In the thirty day follow-up duration the patient did not develop AL and the genitourinary function was not impaired.
